title: Feeling nauseous and how to settle it
excerpt: That queasy, about-to-be-sick feeling has a name in Ayurveda: chhardi. Here is the classical view, and the small kitchen habits that settle a turning stomach.
body_html:
what that queasy feeling is

Most of us know the sensation. A heaviness rises in the chest, the mouth waters a little too much, food smells suddenly become unbearable, and the stomach feels like it wants to turn over. Sometimes it passes. Sometimes it ends in actually being sick. In Ayurveda this whole experience has a name: chhardi, which simply means vomiting or the urge to vomit, with nausea being its quieter early stage.

The classical texts treat nausea and vomiting as one connected problem rather than two. Madhava Nidana, the great manual of diagnosis, devotes a chapter to chhardi and describes how it begins with a feeling of unease and excess salivation before anything actually comes up (Madhava Nidana, chapter on Chhardi). That early watery, restless feeling in the mouth is the body's warning that the stomach is unsettled.

the body wants to throw something out, and forcing it down often makes things worse

the ayurvedic view: an upward push

Ayurveda explains digestion as a downward-moving process. Food should travel down and onward. Nausea and vomiting are what happens when that natural downward flow reverses and the contents of the stomach are pushed upward instead. The classics link this to disturbed doshas, especially aggravated Kapha and Pitta collecting in the stomach, with Vata providing the upward push (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, chapter on Chhardi).

Caraka lists several everyday triggers that any of us would recognise: eating food that disgusts us, eating too much, very oily or very salty food, spoiled or incompatible food, fear and anxiety, and a stomach that is simply overloaded (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana). Ashtanga Hridaya adds that the urge to vomit is the body's own attempt to clear something it does not want, which is why the texts caution against forcibly suppressing a genuine urge to be sick (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, chapter on natural urges). In other words, the body wants to throw something out, and forcing it down often makes things worse.

This is also why the classical first response to mild nausea is gentle, not heavy. The aim is to calm the irritated stomach and let things settle downward again, rather than to push more food or strong medicine into an already unhappy belly.

gentle ways to settle it at home

For ordinary, passing nausea, the granthas favour light, cooling and aromatic measures. Ginger is the classic friend here. Both Caraka and the later compendia praise adrak for kindling digestion and calming the urge to vomit (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, chapter on the benefits of ginger). A little fresh ginger juice with a touch of honey, or simply a thin slice of ginger to suck on, is a time-honoured remedy.

Cardamom, fennel and coriander are the other quiet helpers. Their cooling, fragrant nature soothes a Pitta-irritated stomach, and Bhavaprakasha groups such aromatic seeds among the substances that relieve nausea and bad taste in the mouth (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu section). Chewing a few fennel seeds after a meal, or sipping water in which coriander seeds have soaked, can take the edge off.

Beyond herbs, the simplest habits matter most. Take small sips of water or buttermilk rather than gulping. Rest in fresh air, away from cooking smells. Eat light, dry, easy foods like plain rice, roasted rice flakes or a thin khichdi once the worst passes, and avoid heavy, oily and overly spicy meals for a day. Eat only when you genuinely feel hungry, since loading an unsettled stomach is one of the triggers the texts name.

when to see a doctor

Most nausea is harmless and settles within a day with rest and these gentle measures. But some signs need proper medical attention, not home care. See a doctor or a qualified vaidya if vomiting is severe or will not stop, if you cannot keep any fluids down and feel dizzy or very dry (signs of dehydration), if there is blood in the vomit or it looks like coffee grounds, if there is severe stomach or chest pain, high fever, a bad headache with a stiff neck, or if nausea keeps returning over many days or weeks.

Nausea in pregnancy, in young children, in the elderly, or alongside a known condition also deserves a professional opinion rather than self-treatment. This article is general information drawn from the classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. When in doubt, get checked.

Try This Today

  • a slice of ginger: Suck on a thin slice of fresh ginger, or take a little ginger juice with a touch of honey, at the first turning feeling. Ginger is the classic remedy for calming the urge to vomit.
  • small sips, not gulps: Sip water, plain buttermilk or thin coconut water slowly rather than drinking a lot at once. Gulping an unsettled stomach often brings the nausea straight back.
  • chew a few fennel seeds: After a meal, chew a small pinch of fennel or sip water in which coriander seeds have soaked. Their cooling, aromatic nature soothes a heated, queasy stomach.
  • step away from the smell: Move to fresh air, away from cooking and strong smells, and rest quietly. Food and kitchen odours are a known trigger named in the classics.
  • eat light when it passes: Once the worst settles, return to plain rice, roasted rice flakes or a thin khichdi. Skip oily, heavy and very spicy food for a day, and eat only when truly hungry.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Ginger (अदरक)

The classical first remedy for nausea; a little juice with honey or a slice to suck on calms the urge to vomit.

Fennel (सौंफ)

Cooling and aromatic; chewing a few seeds soothes a heated, queasy stomach after meals.

Coriander (धनिया)

Soaked seeds or their water cool a Pitta-irritated stomach and ease the watery, restless feeling in the mouth.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: That queasy, about-to-be-sick feeling has a name in Ayurveda: chhardi. Here is the classical view, and the small kitchen habits that settle a turning stomach.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Chhardi is the Ayurvedic name for nausea and vomiting, seen as the stomach's contents being pushed upward instead of down.
Common triggers named in the classics are overeating, oily or spoiled food, and fear or anxiety; gentle, light measures calm it best.
Ginger, cardamom, fennel and small sips of fluid are the classical settlers; severe, bloody or persistent vomiting needs a doctor.

glossary_terms.definition: Vomiting, or the urge to vomit; in Ayurveda this covers both nausea and being sick.
The watery, heavy quality in the body; when aggravated in the stomach it adds to the urge to vomit.
The hot, sharp quality linked to digestion; its aggravation makes the stomach irritable and queasy.
The moving, airy quality; here it provides the upward push that reverses normal downward digestion.
Fresh ginger, praised in the classics for kindling digestion and calming nausea.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, chapter on Chhardi (vomiting)
Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, on dietary substances (ginger among the digestives)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, chapter on natural urges

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Chhardi" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Chhardi is defined as: Vomiting, or the urge to vomit; in Ayurveda this covers both nausea and being sick.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to a slice of ginger. Suck on a thin slice of fresh ginger, or take a little ginger juice with a touch of honey, at the first turning feeling. Ginger is the classic remedy for calming the urge to vomit.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Ginger is highly regarded. The classical first remedy for nausea; a little juice with honey or a slice to suck on calms the urge to vomit.
